transactions.name: Bradley Chubb
transactions.analysis: Bradley Chubb's time in Miami showed exactly how impactful he could be when healthy, bringing energy, leadership, and a relentless edge presence to the defense. After arriving in a big midseason trade, he became a tone-setter and delivered a standout 2023 season that helped elevate the pass rush. Injuries made things tougher later on, but his effort and commitment never wavered. As the team turns the page, he leaves as a respected veteran who gave everything he had every time he stepped on the field.
transactions.update: DE Bradley Chubb reached agreement on a three-year, $43.5 million deal worth up to $52.5 million that includes $29 million guaranteed with the Buffalo Bills.

transactions.name: Tyreek Hill
transactions.analysis: Tyreek Hill's time in Miami started with the signature explosiveness and game-breaking speed fans expected, lighting up the field with electric plays that kept defenses on edge. Unfortunately, injuries and off-field distractions limited his impact later on, preventing him from consistently reaching his elite potential. Despite the setbacks, he delivered flashes of brilliance that reminded everyone of the talent he brought to the offense.

transactions.name: James Daniels
transactions.analysis: James Daniels' stint in Miami never materialized as hoped. Healthy for just three plays, he failed to make the impact the team needed on the offensive line, leaving coaches and fans frustrated. Though brief, his time with the Dolphins serves as a cautionary tale about fit and opportunity, and the team will look elsewhere to anchor the trenches moving forward.

transactions.name: Nick Westbrook-Ikhine
transactions.analysis: Despite playing the full season, Nick Westbrook-Ikhine struggled to make a mark, recording just 11 receptions for 89 yards. While he brought professionalism and effort, the on-field production never matched expectations, and he leaves Miami as a player who simply couldn't find his rhythm in the offense.

transactions.name: Liam Eichenberg
transactions.analysis: Hardly a fan favorite, Eichenberg never lived up to his 2nd round selection, but he did add some much needed rotational depth to the offensive line. Unfortunately Liam suffered what appears to be a career ending injury and didn't play a single down in 2025.

transactions.name: Ethan Bonner
transactions.analysis: Bonner was thrust into action last year after a slew of injuries decimated the cornerback room. He was inconsistent at times, but showed enough flashes for the new staff to tender him as an exclusive rights free agent. Because of that designation, he cannot negotiate with other teams, so if he signs the tender he will be back in camp as a Miami Dolphin this summer.

transactions.name: Alec Ingold
transactions.analysis: Ingold was a steady presence in Miami's offense and locker room over the past four seasons, serving as a team captain and earning a Pro Bowl nod in 2023. While fullback usage across the league continues to shrink, his leadership and versatility made him a valuable piece of the Dolphins' culture during Mike McDaniel's early tenure. Moving on likely signals a shift in roster priorities, but Ingold's impact in Miami went well beyond the box score.
transactions.update: The Chargers agreed to terms on a contract with fullback Alec Ingold, the team announced Sunday night (3/8/26). The deal is for two years and up to $7.5 million.

transactions.name: Jason Sanders
transactions.analysis: Sanders leaves Miami as one of the most accomplished kickers in franchise history, holding the team's all-time field goal percentage record and earning First-Team All-Pro honors in 2020. His leg strength and clutch moments helped stabilize the position for several seasons, including multiple playoff runs. The move suggests the Dolphins are ready to reset at kicker, closing the chapter on one of the longest-tenured specialists in recent team history.
transactions.update: Agreed to a one-year deal with the New York Giants.

transactions.name: Tua Tagovailoa
transactions.analysis: After months of buildup, the Dolphins have officially parted ways with quarterback Tua Tagovailoa. The move had been widely expected as Miami navigated a tight salary cap situation, but it still closes the book on one of the most important eras in recent team history. Tagovailoa helped engineer some of the most explosive offenses the franchise has ever seen and was at the center of Miami's offensive identity during the Mike McDaniel years. The decision doesn't come cheap either, but because we designated him a post-June 1st cut, we're able to split the cap hit over the next two years, leaving a $67.4 million cap hit in 2026 and another $31.8 million in 2027 as we turn the page at quarterback.
transactions.update: The Falcons are expected to sign Tua to a one-year deal for the veteran minimum salary, pairing the former Dolphins signal caller with fellow lefty QB Michael Penix.

transactions.name: Minkah Fitzpatrick
transactions.analysis: The Dolphins have traded safety Minkah Fitzpatrick to the New York Jets in exchange for a seventh-round pick originally belonging to the Los Angeles Chargers. The deal ends Fitzpatrick's second stint in Miami after returning to the team last season, and continues a series of moves aimed at creating cap flexibility. While the return is modest, the trade clears additional salary as the Dolphins continue reshaping the roster this offseason.

transactions.name: Malik Willis
transactions.analysis: The Dolphins' new regime has made its first move to stabilize the quarterback position, signing former Packers QB Malik Willis to a three-year, $67.5 million deal with $45 million fully guaranteed. Willis was once considered a draft bust in Tennessee before being traded to Green Bay Packers to back up Jordan Love, where he began to revive his career. In limited action with Green Bay, the young quarterback showed significant growth, generating more free agent interest than many expected this offseason.

transactions.name: Greg Dulcich
transactions.analysis: The Dolphins have re-signed tight end Greg Dulcich to a one-year deal after his late-season emergence. Dulcich joined the team on the practice squad earlier in the year but quickly carved out a role once elevated to the active roster. In limited action he made the most of his opportunities, hauling in 26 receptions for 336 yards and a touchdown, finishing third on the team in receiving. Miami will now look for him to build on that momentum as a reliable target for new quarterback Malik Willis in 2026.

transactions.name: Zane Gonzalez
transactions.analysis: The Dolphins have signed veteran kicker Zane Gonzalez as they look to solidify the position heading into the 2026 season. Gonzalez brings experience from several stops around the league and provides Miami with a reliable leg for both field goals and kickoffs. With both Jason Sanders and Riley Patterson not being retained this offseason, Miami ventured into the Free Agency pool to find their next big leg.

transactions.name: Austin Jackson
transactions.analysis: The Dolphins picked up some breathing room on the cap sheet by restructuring the contract of offensive tackle Austin Jackson. Jackson agreed to a one-year, $7 million reworked deal that includes $5.4 million fully guaranteed and four void years, freeing up about $8.5 million in cap space for Miami this season. Honestly, we figured Jackson might be a cap casualty this offseason, so it's good to see both sides find a way to keep him in Miami while easing the financial hit.

transactions.name: Cameron Goode
transactions.analysis: Goode spent most of last season working his way back from injury but has been a steady special teams contributor when healthy. While he hasn't carved out a major role on defense yet, bringing him back gives Miami a familiar depth piece who knows the system and can contribute in the third phase. Moves like this don't grab headlines, but they help round out the back end of the roster heading into camp.

transactions.name: Willie Gay Jr.
transactions.analysis: The Dolphins have re-signed LB “Big Play” Willie Gay Jr. to a one-year deal, bringing back the little-used linebacker for another run with the new regime. We're big fans of Gay's speed and athleticism and were disappointed former defensive coordinator Anthony Weaver didn't utilize him more last season. With Jeff Hafley now leading the team, we're hoping Gay finally gets the chance to show off that playmaking ability in 2026.

transactions.name: Josh Uche
transactions.analysis: The Dolphins have signed edge rusher Josh Uche to a one-year deal, bringing in a veteran pass-rush piece with real upside. Uche was a second-round pick out of Michigan and quietly built a solid pass-rush resume, posting 21.5 sacks over six seasons with the Patriots, Chiefs and Eagles. His best stretch came in 2022 when he lit it up with 11.5 sacks, and Miami will be hoping he can tap back into that kind of production in a rotational role.

transactions.name: Matthew Butler
transactions.analysis: The Dolphins are bringing back defensive tackle Matthew Butler as veteran depth on the interior. Originally a fifth-round pick by the Raiders out of Tennessee, the 6'3", 305-pound mauler brings quickness and pass-rush juice from the inside. Not a splash move, but a solid depth piece to keep the defensive line rotation fresh.

transactions.name: A.J. Green III
transactions.analysis: Miami is keeping cornerback A. J. Green around as a young depth option in the secondary. The 6'2", 198-pound former Oklahoma State standout has bounced between a few teams since entering the league as an undrafted free agent in 2020, but he's carved out a role on special teams and as a rotational corner.

transactions.name: Darrell Baker
transactions.analysis: The Dolphins have added cornerback Darrell Baker Jr., who comes over after spending time with the Titans. The former Georgia Southern standout went undrafted in 2022 but worked his way into significant playing time, starting 19 games between 2024 and 2025 and racking up 95 tackles with 12 passes defended.

transactions.name: Zayne Anderson
transactions.analysis: Miami adds another special teams contributor with the signing of safety Zayne Anderson. After going undrafted in 2021, Anderson bounced around early in his career before becoming a core special teams player in Green Bay from 2023 to 2025. Expect him to compete for a similar role.

transactions.name: Alex Austin
transactions.analysis: The Dolphins are bringing in cornerback Alex Austin, a former seventh-round pick by the Bills in 2023. Austin most recently spent time with the Patriots, where he developed into a dependable rotational corner and special teams contributor. Another depth piece added to the secondary.

transactions.name: Ben Sims
transactions.analysis: Miami has signed tight end Ben Sims, a blocking-first tight end who's appeared in 45 games over the past three seasons with Green Bay and Minnesota. The former Baylor standout holds the school record for touchdowns by a tight end and should compete for a role as an in-line blocker in Miami's offense, likely replacing the departed Julian Hill.

transactions.name: Lonnie Johnson
transactions.analysis: The Dolphins have added veteran defensive back Lonnie Johnson Jr., bringing some size and physicality to the secondary. The 6'2", 213-pound former second-round pick out of Kentucky has appeared in more than 80 games for the Titans, Saints, Texans, and Raiders. Johnson's versatility to play both safety and corner, along with his special teams experience, makes him a solid depth addition to the defensive back room.

transactions.name: Robert Beal Jr.
transactions.analysis: The Dolphins have added edge rusher Robert Beal Jr., a former fifth-round pick of the 49ers in 2023. The 6'4", 250-pound defender is known for his elite speed, running a 4.47 forty coming out of Georgia, where he won two national championships. Beal has mostly contributed on special teams early in his career, but his athletic upside makes him an intriguing developmental pass-rush piece for Miami.

transactions.name: Riley Patterson
transactions.analysis: The Dolphins are bringing back kicker Riley Patterson, setting up a full-blown kicking competition heading into camp. With veteran Zane Gonzalez also recently signed, Miami suddenly has a battle brewing for the starting job. Patterson gets another shot to claim the role, but this one is clearly going to be decided on the field.

deal.details: Agreed to terms with the New England Patriots.
More O-line help for the Lions: They've agreed to terms with veteran Larry Borom
Signed a two-year, $13 million contract with the Chargers.
Agreed to a one-year deal with the New England Patriots.
Agreed to terms with the Kansas City Chiefs.
Signed a three-year, $9 million deal that includes $5 million fully guaranteed with the Falcons.
Signed a two-year, $3.45 million deal with the Rams.
